title: The Web
tagline: How a physics lab's filing problem became humanity's nervous system.

events.title: ARPANET
events.tagline: The first packet-switched network goes live.
events.content: UCLA and Stanford exchange the first ARPANET message — intended to be *LOGIN*, it crashes after **"LO"**. Packet switching works, and the network that will become the Internet is born.

events.title: Email
events.content: Ray Tomlinson sends the first networked email and picks **@** to separate user from machine. The network's first killer app — decades before the Web itself.

events.title: TCP/IP
events.tagline: One protocol to connect all networks.
events.content: ARPANET switches to **TCP/IP** on "flag day". Networks of networks can now interoperate — this is the moment *the* Internet, lowercase networks becoming one capital-I system, exists.

events.title: The Web is proposed
events.content:
At CERN, Tim Berners-Lee writes **"Information Management: A Proposal"** — hypertext documents, addressed by URL, fetched over the network. His boss scribbles *"vague, but exciting"* on the cover.

*Open this event's timeline for the birth steps.*

children.title: First browser & server
children.content: Berners-Lee writes the first browser/editor (*WorldWideWeb*) and the first server (*httpd*) on a NeXT machine over Christmas 1990. The whole Web runs on one desk.

children.title: The Web goes public
children.content: The project is announced publicly; `info.cern.ch` serves the first website — a page explaining what the World Wide Web is and how to join it.

children.title: W3C founded
children.tagline: The Web gets a steward, not an owner.
children.content: Berners-Lee founds the **World Wide Web Consortium** at MIT and CERN agrees to release the Web's code royalty-free — the decision that kept the Web open.

events.title: Mosaic
events.tagline: The browser that showed images — and everyone the point.
events.content: NCSA **Mosaic** puts images inline with text and installs in minutes. The Web's population explodes from thousands to millions; its authors go on to found Netscape.

events.title: Google
events.tagline: Search makes the Web usable; ads make it an economy.
events.content: Two Stanford students rank pages by their *links* (PageRank), and suddenly the exploding Web is navigable. The data and infrastructure born here later feed the deep-learning era — see [[artificial-intelligence/transformers|Transformers]], invented at Google.

events.title: The social Web
events.tagline: The Web learns who you are.
events.content: Facebook (2004), YouTube (2005), Twitter (2006): the Web's content becomes *us*. Attention becomes the commodity — and the training data this era produces later teaches machines to talk.
